---
title: "Background"
---

This site is a thin layer over data curated by the
[Journal Checker Tool](https://journalcheckertool.org/) (JCT). It helps to know
how that underlying data is assembled, because the linkage between an agreement,
the institutions that take part in it, and the journals it covers is the product
of a voluntary, partly manual, curated process — not an authoritative feed from
publishers.

## The chain of provenance

1. A publisher and a library consortium (or a single institution) negotiate a
   [transformative agreement](https://esac-initiative.org/about/transformative-agreements/).
2. A consortium or institution registers the agreement in the
   [ESAC Transformative Agreement Registry](https://esac-initiative.org/about/transformative-agreements/agreement-registry/),
   a community-curated registry of agreements between publishers and academic
   institutions. Sharing the terms is
   [entirely voluntary](https://esac-initiative.org/about/transformative-agreements/share/),
   and consortium-level agreements are registered at the consortium level rather
   than by each member institution.
3. The JCT data-management team
   [crawls the ESAC registry weekly](https://www.coalition-s.org/blog/enabling-accurate-results-within-the-journal-checker-tool/).
   When it finds a new agreement, it reaches out to the consortium to obtain the
   list of titles covered and which institutions have access, then publishes the
   result in the JCT.
4. The JCT exposes this as CC0
   ["Transformative Agreements Public Data"](https://journalcheckertool.org/transformative-agreements/)
   and an [API](https://journalcheckertool.org/apidocs).
5. This site consumes that public data and matches agreements to TU9
   universities by ROR identifier.

## How the JCT curates it

The crucial step is the third one. ESAC alone does not yield a clean list of
named participating institutions: registration is at the consortium level, and
contributors submit
whatever level of detail they are comfortable providing.
The participating-institution list is therefore obtained by the JCT team
contacting consortia directly and curating the result by hand — it is not
scraped from publishers.

There is also
[no fixed one-to-one relationship](https://www.coalition-s.org/blog/introducing-new-search-features-in-the-journal-checker-tool-for-transformative-agreements/)
between an agreement and its journal and institution lists: a single agreement
can give different institutions access to different sets of titles, which the
JCT records in its own per-agreement sheets.

Maintenance is a distributed partnership, and the JCT does not own most of the
underlying data. The transformative-agreement records combine the agreements
from the ESAC registry with the participating institutions' ROR identifiers; the
journals themselves are listed by name and ISSN in the consortia's own
per-agreement sheets. The JCT's
[other data sources](https://journalcheckertool.org/data-sources/) serve the
wider tool rather than this dataset — Crossref backs its journal-title search and
DOAJ its separate fully-open-access compliance check. Corrections to the
agreement index go through the JCT helpdesk, while portfolio-level changes are
submitted back to the original data source.

## What the dates and identifiers mean

Because curation happens in periodic review cycles rather than as a live feed,
the dates in the data are **observational**. Upstream, the JCT records
when it first and last observed
a journal or institution to be part of an agreement — not the actual date it
joined or left, which the JCT does not have.

Journals are identified by their print and online ISSNs, and institutions by
[ROR identifier](https://ror.org/) where available — the same ROR linkage this
site relies on to match an agreement to a TU9 university.

## Caveats

The coverage figures on this site inherit the limits of their source.
Completeness depends on consortia choosing to register their agreements and to
respond to the JCT's requests, and some register without disclosing every
detail: the public data dump is a voluntary effort built on the contracts
consortia have publicly disclosed, crowd-sourced from agreement documents and
consortia ([Jahn 2025](https://doi.org/10.1162/qss_a_00348)). Only agreements
that have been
[fully reviewed and confirmed Plan S compliant](https://journalcheckertool.org/transformative-agreements/)
appear in the public index, and an expired agreement is
[dropped about three months after it ends](https://journalcheckertool.org/faq/)
— so the data is a curated snapshot, not a complete or historical record.
Independent validation is nonetheless encouraging: when an open method built on
the JCT and OpenAlex was checked against real publisher data, 89% of the
agreement matches it made were correct (precision; 2,054 of 2,304), though it
missed about half of the true cases (53% recall)
([de Jonge, Kramer & Sondervan 2025](https://doi.org/10.1162/qss.a.24)). Treat
the numbers here as a precise but conservative lower bound on TU9 participation,
not a guarantee of every title or institution covered.

Finally, this site reflects only the JCT's transformative-agreement data. It is
**not a substitute for a full JCT compliance check**, which evaluates a specific
funder, institution, journal and author role — and which also recognises other
routes to compliance such as fully open-access journals, self-archiving with
rights retention, and transformative journals. Eligibility is also narrower than
an agreement's title list suggests: these agreements cover OA only for articles
where the TU9 author is the corresponding author — in the current data, every
one is flagged this way (kept as the `ca_only` field in the downloadable
agreements CSV).

## References

- de Jonge, H., Kramer, B., & Sondervan, J. (2025). Tracking transformative
  agreements through open metadata: Method and validation using Dutch Research
  Council NWO funded papers. *Quantitative Science Studies*, 6, 1215–1227.
  <https://doi.org/10.1162/qss.a.24>
- Jahn, N. (2025). How open are hybrid journals included in transformative
  agreements? *Quantitative Science Studies*, 6, 242–262.
  <https://doi.org/10.1162/qss_a_00348>
